pricing.source: OpenAI API pricing, standard short-context rates, 2026-06-23

hardChecks.label: Citation source ids
hardChecks.expectation: Every cited source id exists in the source packet

hardChecks.label: Cost metadata
hardChecks.expectation: estimatedUsd is present when pricing is configured

hardChecks.label: Token metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Input, cached-input, and output token counts are coherent

hardChecks.label: Provider and model metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Provider, model, and prompt version are present

hardChecks.label: Judge input boundary
hardChecks.expectation: Judge prompt omits eval-only object keys and holdout tuning fields

hardChecks.label: Judge claim coverage
hardChecks.expectation: LLM judge returns exactly one judgment for every briefing claim

claimJudgments.claimText: Reviewer rotation reduced median review wait time from 19 hours to 13 hours, but P90 remains 46 hours due to complex changes waiting on the same two senior reviewers.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 explicitly reports median improved 19h→13h after rotation and P90 is 46h because complex changes often wait for the same two senior reviewers.

claimJudgments.claimText: Docs and configuration changes moved faster after rotation, while cross-service/shared-service changes still experienced long waits due to scarce/overloaded expert reviewers.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 says documentation and configuration reviews moved quickly after rotation; changes touching shared services still waited on scarce reviewers.

claimJudgments.claimText: Queue tail latency is primarily an ownership-routing problem (especially for shared services), not only a staffing problem.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 recommends treating tail latency as an ownership-routing problem, not only a staffing problem; it ties the issue to shared-service/complex changes.

claimJudgments.claimText: Reviewers support auto-assignment for routine changes but distrust automated approvals, especially when tests are flaky or ownership is unclear.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 states reviewers like auto-assignment for routine changes and distrust bot approvals when tests are flaky or ownership unclear.

claimJudgments.claimText: Reviewers want risk labels that explain why a change is safe to fast-track (ownership metadata, test health, file categories) and want the label to be challengeable before merge.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 says reviewers asked for risk labels explaining low risk (ownership metadata, test health, file categories) and that a fast-track label would be useful only if it could be challenged before merge.

recommendationJudgment.explanation: Recommendations are consistent with packet themes: reduce P90 via ownership routing (S1, S5), keep automation to organizing/suggesting not bypass approvals (S4, S6), constrain fast-track to docs/config with healthy tests and challengeable labels (S2, S6), and require owning-team review for cross-service changes due to prior incidents (S3, S6). No claims of capabilities beyond sources.

citationSupport.note: S1 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S2 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.

notes: Hybrid evaluator output: deterministic hard checks plus structured LLM judge evidence. Manual spot checks are still required before claiming product improvement.
